title: Extended curing for slow-finishing or patchy compost
subtitle: How to identify immature compost and what to do about it
excerpt: Compost that looks finished but still contains recognisable material, smells musty, or causes plant stress when applied is immature. Extended curing in a covered heap for 4 to 12 weeks resolves most immaturity. This covers the tests for maturity and the curing conditions that accelerate finishing.
sourceNotes: USDA composting guide on maturity indicators. Itävaara, Venelampi et al. research on compost maturity testing methods. RHS and Garden Organic advice on compost finishing.

glossaryTerms.definition: A passive composting method where material decomposes slowly at near-ambient temperatures, typically over 6 to 18 months.

content.text: Immature compost applied to soil or pots can harm plants in two ways. First, it keeps decomposing in the soil, using up nitrogen from the soil solution to feed microbial activity. This cuts nitrogen available to plant roots and causes yellowing, especially in brassicas. Second, it can contain plant-toxic compounds from anaerobic decomposition, such as acetic and butyric acid. These inhibit germination and root growth.
Smell test: finished compost smells pleasantly earthy, similar to moist woodland soil. Sour, fermenty, or ammonia-like smell indicates immaturity. No-smell or faint mushroom smell is acceptable.
Visual check: no more than 5 to 10% of the volume should be recognisable as original material. Tough stems, woody pieces, and eggshells may remain but should be in the minority.
Bag test: seal a double handful in a plastic bag for 3 days. If the bag bulges from gas production, the compost is still actively decomposing.
Germination test: fill a small container and sow 10 cress or radish seeds. 80% germination (8 of 10) after 5 days is the standard pass threshold for finished compost.
Temperature test: finished compost no longer heats. If the pile reaches 40°C within 2 days of adding water and turning, it is still actively decomposing.

If the compost fails any of the tests above, move it to a covered curing bay: a pile on open ground covered with a tarpaulin or heavy-duty polythene. The cover prevents wetting and drying cycles from disrupting the final decomposition phase. Turn the curing pile once, mix through any unfinished patches with the more finished material, and leave for 4 to 12 weeks.
that has been in the heap for less than a year typically needs 4 to 8 weeks of curing; hot compost that did not maintain temperature long enough needs 4 to 6 weeks.

If the compost fails the maturity tests but there is an immediate use for it, it can be applied to soil and dug in 4 to 6 weeks before planting, giving time for further curing in place. Do not use immature compost as a seed-sowing medium, as a top dressing around seedlings, or as a mulch around acid-sensitive shrubs. It is safe to use as a mulch on empty beds or under established shrubs and trees where it will continue curing in place.
